stem: A 5-year-old previously well girl is brought by her parents to a remote GP clinic 90 minutes from the nearest base hospital. She woke 6 hours ago with sudden fever and a headache. Over the last hour she has become drowsy and developed a rash on her trunk. The retrieval service has been activated; estimated transfer time is 75 minutes. IV access is established and bloods drawn.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 154, RR 36, SpO2 95% RA, BP 78/40, T 39.6°C, capillary refill 4 s, GCS E3 V4 M5
data_table.Examination: Cool peripheries, multiple purpuric lesions over trunk and legs, mild neck stiffness, pupils 3 mm equal and reactive, no focal deficits, no positive Kernig's sign
data_table.Investigations: Blood cultures drawn; VBG pH 7.22, lactate 6.4, BSL 4.8; FBC and coagulation pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step before retrieval?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until cultures result so subsequent treatment is fully culture-directed
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in suspected meningococcal sepsis is associated with markedly increased mortality; cultures should not delay therapy in critically unwell children. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal disease)

options.text: Administer intravenous ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg with crystalloid 20 mL/kg fluid bolus
options.rationale: Suspected meningococcal sepsis with hypotension and shock requires immediate IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg plus aggressive fluid resuscitation; do not delay for cultures or transfer. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal disease; eTG)

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture before any antibiotic dose to confirm meningococcal infection
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated in shock, raised intracranial pressure or coagulopathy; antibiotic delay for LP is harmful. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous vancomycin 15 mg/kg alone and observe for response over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Vancomycin does not cover Neisseria meningitidis adequately as monotherapy; third-generation cephalosporin is first-line in AU. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Administer intramuscular benzylpenicillin 50 mg/kg into the lateral thigh region
options.rationale: IM benzylpenicillin is reserved for retrieval scenarios where IV access cannot be obtained; IV ceftriaxone is preferred when access is available and gives broader cover. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal disease)

explanation.summary: Suspected meningococcal sepsis is a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate empirical IV antibiotics and fluid resuscitation, ideally within 30 minutes of recognition. Australian first-line is IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg (max 4 g) plus 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus repeated as required, with early vasopressors if fluid-refractory shock. Cultures and LP must NOT delay treatment in a shocked child. IM benzylpenicillin 50 mg/kg is the prehospital alternative when IV access is not possible during retrieval. Notifiable disease - notify public health within 24 hours.
key_points: IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg (max 4 g) once daily - first-line for invasive meningococcal disease.
Fluid resuscitation 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, reassess, repeat up to 40 to 60 mL/kg.
Vasopressors (noradrenaline) early for fluid-refractory shock.
Notifiable disease - notify public health within 24 hours.
Close contact chemoprophylaxis: ciprofloxacin (adult) or ceftriaxone (children, pregnant).
explanation.pearls: In meningococcal septic shock, classical signs of meningism (neck stiffness, Kernig's) may be absent or develop late; purpuric rash plus tachycardia or capillary refill over 3 seconds in a febrile child is sufficient for empirical antibiotics.

stem: A 4-day-old term male infant who was discharged at 36 hours after an unremarkable postnatal course is brought back to the emergency department after 24 hours of progressive lethargy, refusing feeds and now 6 hours of seizure-like episodes. He was breastfeeding well at discharge. Parents are non-consanguineous and have a healthy 3-year-old daughter. No maternal infection identified.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 70 with tachypnoea episodes, SpO2 96% RA, T 36.3°C, BSL 3.6 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Hypotonic infant, poor suck, intermittent tonic posturing, mild jaundice, no rash, anterior fontanelle soft, pupils sluggish
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.51 pCO2 22 HCO3 17; lactate 1.8; ammonia 380 micromol/L; UEC normal; FBC normal; CRP 4 mg/L; urine ketones negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin and gentamicin and arrange urgent lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Sepsis workup is appropriate but markedly elevated ammonia in a non-acidotic infant points to urea cycle disorder requiring immediate metabolic intervention; antibiotics alone do not address the toxic metabolite. (Source: RCH CPG: Metabolic emergencies)

options.text: Refer urgently to surgical team for exploratory laparotomy and bowel obstruction workup
options.rationale: Surgical pathology unlikely with this metabolic picture; hyperammonaemia direction is genetic metabolic disease, not surgical. (Source: RCH CPG: Metabolic emergencies)

options.text: Initiate phototherapy and supplemental enteral feeds to prevent further weight loss
options.rationale: Phototherapy is for hyperbilirubinaemia not metabolic encephalopathy; enteral feeds with protein would worsen ammonia. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Administer intravenous phenobarbital 20 mg/kg loading dose and observe for 24 hours
options.rationale: Treating seizures without addressing hyperammonaemia is futile; sodium phenobarbital may worsen sedation without lowering ammonia. (Source: ASIEM 2021)

options.text: Stop protein intake, start IV 10 percent dextrose at 1.5x maintenance and prepare for haemodialysis
options.rationale: Ammonia above 200 micromol/L in a newborn with respiratory alkalosis (hyperammonaemia-driven hyperventilation) without ketoacidosis is urea cycle defect until proven otherwise: stop protein, give high glucose to reverse catabolism, transfer for emergency haemodialysis (ammonia over 300 to 500). (Source: RCH CPG: Metabolic emergencies; ASIEM guideline)

explanation.summary: A previously well newborn deteriorating in the first week of life with feeding refusal, lethargy, hypotonia and seizures must be considered for inborn errors of metabolism alongside sepsis. The classic urea cycle defect picture is hyperammonaemia (over 200 micromol/L), respiratory alkalosis from central hyperventilation, normal lactate, and no ketoacidosis. Initial management: stop protein, start 10 percent dextrose at 1.5x maintenance to reverse catabolism, IV sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate (ammonia scavengers), arginine; arrange emergency haemodialysis if ammonia exceeds 300 to 500 micromol/L or rapidly rising.
key_points: Suspect IEM in any unwell newborn with no clear sepsis source.
Hyperammonaemia + respiratory alkalosis + no acidosis = urea cycle defect.
Hyperammonaemia + metabolic acidosis + ketosis = organic acidaemia.
Stop protein, start 10 percent dextrose at 1.5x maintenance, IV scavengers.
Haemodialysis if ammonia over 300 to 500 or rapidly rising despite medical therapy.
explanation.pearls: Carglumic acid (N-carbamylglutamate) is the antidote for N-acetylglutamate synthase deficiency and for propionic and methylmalonic acidaemia presenting with hyperammonaemia; consider as part of empirical treatment under metabolic team guidance.

subtopic_detail: ALL with high white cell count tumour lysis
stem: A 6-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with 4 weeks of fatigue, easy bruising and bilateral knee pain. She had a viral illness 2 weeks ago. Her parents have no medical concerns about her sister. She has had 3 nosebleeds in the last week and looks pale. The intern has just received the FBC result and is awaiting paediatric oncology review.

data_table.Examination: Pale, scattered petechiae on legs, splenomegaly 4 cm below costal margin, hepatomegaly 2 cm, no lymphadenopathy on neck, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Hb 67 g/L, WCC 178 x10^9/L (blasts 90 percent), platelets 18 x10^9/L; LDH 4200 U/L; uric acid 580 micromol/L; potassium 4.8 mmol/L; phosphate 2.4 mmol/L; calcium 2.18 mmol/L; creatinine 78 micromol/L; urine output 1.2 mL/kg/h

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management before starting chemotherapy?

options.text: Begin platelet transfusion targeting count above 50 x10^9/L before any procedure or therapy
options.rationale: Platelet threshold for prophylaxis is 10 x10^9/L stable or 20 x10^9/L febrile; transfusion to 50 is for active bleeding or invasive procedure. (Source: NBA blood management module 6)

options.text: Start oral allopurinol 300 mg twice daily and proceed with chemotherapy in 24 hours
options.rationale: Allopurinol is used in low-risk TLS prophylaxis only; high-risk WCC over 100 needs rasburicase plus hyperhydration. (Source: COSA tumour lysis 2022)

options.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed red blood cells to correct her haemoglobin then commence vincristine
options.rationale: Aggressive transfusion in hyperleukocytic ALL may increase blood viscosity and worsen leukostasis; restrictive transfusion is preferred. (Source: NBA blood management module 6)

options.text: Administer intravenous frusemide 1 mg/kg to enhance uric acid excretion via the kidneys
options.rationale: Diuretics are not first-line in TLS prophylaxis and may worsen dehydration; alkalinisation is no longer recommended either. (Source: COSA 2022)

options.text: Commence intravenous hyperhydration with normal saline at 3 L/m2/day plus rasburicase
options.rationale: WCC over 100 x10^9/L with high LDH and elevated uric acid in ALL is high tumour lysis risk; AU guidance is hyperhydration at 3 L/m2/day plus rasburicase (urate oxidase) within 4 hours of diagnosis before chemotherapy. (Source: COSA tumour lysis 2022; eviQ)

explanation.summary: Paediatric acute lymphoblastic leukaemia with hyperleukocytosis (WCC over 100 x10^9/L) carries high risk of tumour lysis syndrome (TLS): hyperuricaemia, hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia and secondary hypocalcaemia with acute kidney injury. AU prophylaxis is hyperhydration with normal saline at 3 L/m2/day (without potassium) plus rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV for high-risk patients (over 100 WCC, bulky disease, high LDH, baseline uric acid over 480 micromol/L). Allopurinol is reserved for low or intermediate risk. Strict input-output monitoring, 4-hourly electrolytes, and avoidance of aggressive transfusion in hyperleukocytic disease.
key_points: High-risk TLS: B-ALL with WCC over 100, Burkitt's, large bulky disease.
Prophylaxis: hyperhydration 3 L/m2/day no potassium + rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg.
Allopurinol for low/intermediate risk.
No alkalinisation (worsens calcium phosphate precipitation).
Monitor 4-hourly UEC, calcium, phosphate, uric acid and urine output.
explanation.pearls: Rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency due to risk of severe haemolytic anaemia and methaemoglobinaemia; AU guidelines do not require routine pre-treatment G6PD testing in children of European ancestry but consider in at-risk populations.

stem: A 19-year-old primiparous mother is admitted to the postnatal ward 12 hours after vaginal birth of a 3050 g term male infant. She is smoking 10 cigarettes daily, has refused antenatal smoking-cessation referrals, and tells the midwife she plans to bed-share with her baby because both her sisters did. The infant is feeding adequately at the breast. Her partner is also a smoker and reports binge alcohol use at weekends.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 110/68, HR 72; infant T 36.6, HR 138, RR 42, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Examination: Maternal: well-appearing, comfortable; infant: pink, active, suck strong, no abnormalities
data_table.Investigations: Routine newborn examination normal; SWISH (newborn hearing) pending; Australian newborn screening test taken

lead_in: Which approach best reduces this infant's risk of sudden unexpected death in infancy (SUDI)?

options.text: Reassure the mother that bed-sharing carries no additional risk in breastfed infants under 6 months
options.rationale: Bed-sharing with smoker parents or after alcohol use markedly increases SUDI risk; reassurance is inaccurate and harmful. (Source: Red Nose Australia 2022)

options.text: Initiate child protection notification as bed-sharing constitutes mandatory reporting alone
options.rationale: Bed-sharing alone is not a child protection trigger unless other significant risk factors and counselling refusal; notification without engagement misses opportunity. (Source: RCH CPG: Child protection)

options.text: Recommend the infant sleep prone with rolled towels for positioning to prevent reflux
options.rationale: Prone sleeping increases SUDI risk fivefold; supine has been first-line since the AU back-to-sleep campaign. (Source: Red Nose Australia 2022)

options.text: Document SUDI risks, counsel on supine sleep in a cot, refer to smoking cessation supports
options.rationale: Documented evidence-based counselling on safe sleep (supine, on a firm flat surface in a cot in the parents' room, no bed-sharing with smokers or after alcohol), plus active referral to smoking cessation and social work, addresses modifiable risks. (Source: Red Nose Australia 2022)

options.text: Provide a pamphlet on safe sleep and discharge without further engagement before postnatal day 1
options.rationale: Written information alone does not change behaviour; structured counselling and warm referrals to cessation supports are required for at-risk families. (Source: Red Nose Australia 2022)

explanation.summary: Sudden unexpected death in infancy (SUDI) is the leading cause of postneonatal death in Australia. Modifiable risk factors include prone or side sleeping, soft bedding, head covering, bed-sharing especially with smokers or after alcohol use, prematurity and parental smoking. Red Nose Australia (formerly SIDS and Kids) safe sleep recommendations form the AU standard: supine sleep position, firm flat mattress in a safe cot, in parents' room for 6 to 12 months, no soft toys or pillows, no bed-sharing with smokers, and smoke-free environment. Postnatal counselling should be tailored, documented, and linked with cessation supports.
key_points: Supine sleep on firm flat surface in a safe cot.
In parents' room for 6 to 12 months (room-share, not bed-share).
Avoid bed-sharing with smokers, after alcohol or sedatives, or with preterm infants.
Smoke-free environment - active referral to cessation (Quitline 13 7848).
Avoid head covering, soft bedding, sleep positioners, drop-side cots.
explanation.pearls: Risk of SUDI peaks between 2 and 4 months of age and remains elevated to 12 months; safe sleep counselling at every newborn home visit by child and family health nurses is part of the AU Universal Postnatal Care Package.

stem: A 5-week-old firstborn male infant is referred from his GP with 10 days of progressive non-bilious vomiting, now projectile after every feed for 48 hours. He has lost weight since his 2-week check. The GP suspected gastroenteritis but vomiting persists despite oral rehydration. The on-call paediatric surgical team has been notified for likely pyloric stenosis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 48, SpO2 100% RA, T 36.5°C, capillary refill 3 s, weight 3.6 kg (birthweight 3.4 kg)
data_table.Examination: Lethargic but rousable, sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, palpable olive in epigastrium, visible peristaltic waves, abdomen soft and non-distended
data_table.Investigations: Na 128 mmol/L, K 2.9 mmol/L, Cl 84 mmol/L, HCO3 36 mmol/L, urea 11.8 mmol/L, creatinine 65 micromol/L; capillary VBG pH 7.52 pCO2 56; abdominal US: pylorus 4.5 mm thick, 18 mm long

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Transfer immediately to theatre for Ramstedt pyloromyotomy under general anaesthesia today
options.rationale: Pyloromyotomy is the definitive treatment but is delayed until biochemical correction; operating with hypochloraemic alkalosis risks postoperative apnoea. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Insert nasogastric tube on continuous suction and withhold any intravenous fluid until surgery
options.rationale: NG drainage worsens chloride and potassium loss; aggressive IV correction must occur, not be withheld, and NG decompression is selective. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Commence 0.45 percent saline plus 5 percent dextrose with potassium 20 mmol per litre
options.rationale: Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with dehydration is corrected with isotonic or near-isotonic chloride-rich crystalloid plus potassium replacement; 0.45 percent saline with dextrose is RCH first-line for moderate dehydration in this scenario. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Administer intravenous 8.4 percent sodium bicarbonate to correct apparent metabolic alkalosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate worsens alkalosis; therapy targets restoration of chloride and potassium via crystalloid not bicarbonate. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Trial thickened oral feeds with oral rehydration solution and reassess clinically in 6 hours
options.rationale: Oral rehydration cannot resolve pyloric obstruction; vomiting will continue and electrolyte disturbance worsen. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

explanation.summary: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis classically presents in firstborn males between 3 and 6 weeks with progressive non-bilious projectile vomiting, weight loss and a palpable olive. The biochemical signature is hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from loss of gastric HCl. Definitive treatment is Ramstedt pyloromyotomy but must be delayed until biochemical correction to prevent postoperative apnoea and hypoventilation from persistent alkalosis. AU first-line resuscitation is 0.45 to 0.9 percent saline with 5 percent dextrose plus potassium chloride 20 to 40 mmol/L, targeting normalisation of chloride above 100 and HCO3 below 30 before theatre.
key_points: Classic presentation: firstborn male, 3 to 6 weeks, projectile non-bilious vomiting, olive.
Biochemistry: hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria.
Confirm with ultrasound: pylorus over 4 mm thick, length over 16 mm.
Resuscitate first: chloride-rich crystalloid + potassium until Cl over 100, HCO3 under 30.
Pyloromyotomy after biochemical correction; oral feeds restart 6 hours postop.
explanation.pearls: Paradoxical aciduria in pyloric stenosis - kidneys preferentially conserve sodium over hydrogen ions when severely depleted, producing acidic urine despite systemic alkalosis. Urinary chloride under 10 mmol/L distinguishes from chloride-resistant alkalosis.
